one_line_takeaway: Token-level "sanitization" that surgically strips AI-directed instructions from tool outputs cuts indirect-prompt-injection ASR by 7-19x across five benchmarks while, unlike blocking detectors, preserving agent utility.
key_finding: CommandSans* reduces AgentDojo Important-Instructions ASR from 34.67% to 3.48% (GPT-4o), 4.95% to 0.74% (Claude Sonnet 3.7), and 16.02% to 0.84% (Gemini 2.5 Pro) — roughly 10x/7x/19x — with no significant utility loss (e.g., GPT-4o under-attack utility rises from 46.89% to 63.75%). On BIPIA (GPT-4o) overall ASR drops from 57.1% to 13.8% (CommandSans), far below the PI Detector's 40.7%. On InjecAgent Enhanced (GPT-4) CommandSans* cuts total ASR from 46.4% to 4.6%. On Agent Security Bench it achieves injection-removal rate >94% (up to 97.58%) with near-baseline utility. On SEP it lowers ASR from 68.25% to 5.65% while preserving content (BERT-Score ~0.94, ROUGE-L ~0.92). In the human red-teaming study ASR falls from 34.37% (no defense) to 1.69% (CommandSans*) at ~99% utility; only two bypass strategies were found: tokenization manipulation (later fixed by CommandSans* augmentation) and semantic reframing (1/360 submissions, ~1%), which the authors acknowledge as an unresolved limitation of instruction-based detection.
core_contribution: The paper introduces CommandSans, a defense against indirect prompt injection in LLM agents that reframes the problem from sample-level detection to token-level "instruction sanitization." Instead of classifying whole tool outputs as malicious/benign (which suffers high false-positive rates and blocks the agent), the authors train a small encoder-only model to tag, at the token level, any span that constitutes an instruction directed at an AI system, then surgically remove those tokens from tool outputs before they enter the LLM's context. The guiding principle is the classical security maxim that "data should not contain executable instructions"; malicious injections are caught as a byproduct of removing all AI-directed instructions. A key claimed advantage is that the sanitizer can be trained purely from ordinary instruction-tuning corpora (relabeled at token granularity), avoiding reliance on synthetic/out-of-distribution prompt-injection datasets, and is non-blocking, calibration-free, low-latency, and context-agnostic. Two variants are presented: CommandSans (trained on non-malicious instruction data only) and CommandSans* (adds synthetic malicious data plus data augmentation to resist tokenizer attacks).
threat_model: Indirect (not direct) prompt injection against tool-using LLM agents. The user is trusted; the attacker cannot access the model interface, alter training, or perform direct prompt injection. Instead the attacker tampers with untrusted external data the agent consumes via tools — websites, emails, documents, API/database/search responses — embedding instructions that hijack the agent to cause data exfiltration, destructive/unauthorized actions, or task manipulation. The defense sits between tool outputs and the LLM context and must be low-latency with a very low false-positive rate. A secondary concern explicitly modeled is second-order injection targeting the defense itself, which motivates using a small non-instruction-following classifier rather than prompting a capable LLM.
limitations: The authors acknowledge semantic reframing (rephrasing malicious instructions as e.g. "third-party compliance rules") bypasses instruction-based detection and remains unsolved (~1% red-team success). Performance is weaker on structured-data domains not matching the training distribution: on BIPIA Code QA ASR stays high (~33-35%) because malicious code, distinct from malicious instructions, remains after sanitization; Table QA also improves less. The approach only removes AI-directed instructions, so injections that achieve harm without explicit instructions are out of scope. On InjecAgent the simple attack prefix makes the blocking PI Detector score lower ASR (0.9%) than CommandSans, though PI Detector destroys utility (near-0% across benchmarks, e.g. ASB utility 0). Some metrics are proxies (Injection Removal Rate for ASB; similarity scores for SEP) rather than direct end-to-end ASR, and ASB ASR figures are marked as estimated. The GPT-4 LLM-labeling pipeline has ~5% mislabeling and is itself vulnerable to second-order injection (shown in appendix), which is why a non-instruction-following model is used at deployment.

datasets_used: AgentDojo (97 tasks, 629 security test cases; Important Instructions attack)
BIPIA (email QA, table QA, summarization, code QA)
InjecAgent (1,054 test cases; Enhanced attack setting)
Agent Security Bench / ASB (Observable Prompt Injection Combined attack)
Training: ~5,000 synthetic MCP JSON tool outputs from GitHub tool descriptions
quantitative_results: AgentDojo GPT-4o ASR 34.67% -> 3.48% (CommandSans*), ~10x; utility under attack 46.89% -> 63.75%
ASB (GPT-4o) Injection Removal Rate 94.94% (CommandSans) / 97.58% (CommandSans*); estimated ASR ~1.25% / 0.00% vs 70.25% no-defense
Human red-teaming ASR 34.37% -> 2.82% (CommandSans) -> 1.69% (CommandSans*); utility ~99%; semantic-reframing bypass 1/360 (~1%)
Model: XLM-RoBERTa-base, 279M parameters; CommandSans trained 3 epochs, CommandSans* 5 epochs
baselines_compared: PI Detector (blocking SOTA prompt-injection detector, Sentinel / Ivry & Nahum 2025)
PI Sanitization (ablated variant trained to detect malicious injections directly rather than all AI-directed instructions)
